coredata.dc:title: Blood-brain barrier penetrating carbon dots with intrinsic anti-inflammatory and drug-loading properties
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xianming
Yu, Qinghua
Zhou, Ping
Yang, Shiyu
Xia, Jiashan
Deng, Tao
Yu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: The blood-brain barrier (BBB) is the major obstacle limiting the reach of therapeutic drugs into the brain. Herein, an aspirin-based anti-inflammatory replenisher (aspCD) was fabricated by carbonizing aspirin to deliver drugs into the brain visually. The as-prepared aspCD combined the BBB-penetrating and anti-inflammatory effects of aspirin with the fluorescent and drug-loading properties of carbon dots (CDs), thereby delivering therapeutic drugs into the brain and acting as imaging agent as well as anti-inflammatory replenisher. In vivo experiments of mice and zebrafish revealed that fluorescence aspCD could effectively penetrate BBB. In vitro and in vivo inflammatory models demonstrated that aspCD could be regarded as an excellent anti-inflammatory replenisher. In addition, as a functional carrier, aspCD was proved to be capable of loading drugs with different polarity. In summary, carbonization of active precursors (therapeutic drugs) into CDs could be a promising strategy to achieve the loading and visualization of drugs as well as the retainment of their biological activities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti-inflammatory replenisher
Bioimaging
Blood-brain barrier
Carbon dots
Visualized drug carrier

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The basic characteristic of aspCD. (a) The photographs of aspCD solution and powder. (b) TEM of aspCD. (c) Particle size of aspCD analyzed from TEM image. (d) Zeta potential of aspirin in H2O and aspCD in H2O and PBS. (e) The FTIR spectra of aspirin and aspCD. (f) The emission spectrum of aspCD at different excitation wavelengths. (g) The excitation spectrum (emitted at 400 nm) and emission spectrum (excited at 365 nm) of aspCD in aqueous solution. Inset: The photographs of aspCD under sunlight and UV light. (h) Fluorescence decay of the aspCD dilute aqueous solution at 25 °C (excitation at 360 nm).

The fluorescence stability of aspCD. The fluorescence change of aspCD mixed with PBS (a), 1640 cell culture medium (b) and serum (c) for 24 h. The fluorescence spectra of aspCD at different pH values ranging from 4 to 9 (d), different concentration of KCl ranging from 0 to 4 M (e), and different irradiation time of an ultraviolet lamp ranging from 0 to 5 h (f). The fluorescence intensity at 410 nm of aspCD at different pH values ranging from 4 to 9 (g), different concentration of KCl ranging from 0 to 4 M (h), and different irradiation time of an ultraviolet lamp ranging from 0 to 5 h (i).

Bioimaging of aspCD. (a) The uptake and in vitro fluorescence imaging of aspCD with RAW264.7. (b) The fluorescence signals of aspCD at different time after subcutaneous injection at the concentration of 25 mg/kg.

The BBB-penetrating capacity of aspCD. (a) The fluorescence distribution of aspCD in the brains at different times. (b) The fluorescence distribution of aspCD in the brains and main organs after intravenous injection of PBS and aspCD at the concentration of 25 mg/kg for 24 h. (c) The fluorescence ex vivo imaging and relevant section of the brains. (d) The quantitative analysis of aspCD distributed in brain and main organs after intravenous injection of aspCD at the concentration of 25 mg/kg for 24 h. (e) The amount of aspCD distributed in brain and main organs and its ratio in injection dosage after intravenous injection of aspCD at 10 mg/mL for 24 h. (f) The fluorescence imaging of zebrafish co-incubated with PBS or aspCD.

The anti-inflammatory effect of aspCD. (a) Western blot of IL-1β and TNF-α expression at 24 h after treated with aspirin or aspCD at the concentration of 50 or 100 μg/mL. (b) The schematic timeline of in vivo anti-inflammation evaluation and the anti-inflammatory mechanisms. (c) H&E staining of anti-inflammatory effect of aspirin and aspCD.

The therapeutic effect of aspCD on brain inflammatory diseases. (a) The schematic timeline of anti-inflammation evaluation. Immunohistochemistry of the expression of related inflammatory factors (b) and its quantitative analysis (c).

Drug loading and release properties of aspCD. (a) The process of loading various drugs onto aspCD. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of aspCD loaded with RhB (b), DOX (d) and rhein (f) (Inset: The photographs of aspCD (1), loaded drugs (2) and drug loaded aspCD (3)). The fluorescence spectra of aspCD loaded with RhB (c), DOX (e) and rhein (g). (h) The release kinetics curves of RhB from aspCD@RhB at the pH of 7.4 and 5.5 in PBS. (i) The release kinetics curves of DOX from aspCD@DOX at the pH of 7.4 and 5.5 in PBS. (j) The release kinetics curves of rhein from aspCD@rhein at the pH of 7.4 and 5.5 in PBS.

The schematic representative of the synthesis and application of aspCD. (a) The process of synthesizing aspCD and loading model drugs (I). (b) The as-prepared aspCD crosses the BBB and performs the effects of bioimaging (II) and anti-inflammation (III).
Calculation of fluorescence quantum yield of aspCD.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The bold refers to the calculated fluorescence quantum yield of aspCD.

head.ce:title: Blood-brain barrier penetrating carbon dots with intrinsic anti-inflammatory and drug-loading properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Pharmaceutical Metabolism Research, Chongqing 400016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Pharmaceutical Metabolism Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Pharmaceutical Metabolism Research, Chongqing 400016, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Pharmacodynamic Evaluation Engineering Technology Research Center, Chongqing 400016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Pharmacodynamic Evaluation Engineering Technology Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Pharmacodynamic Evaluation Engineering Technology Research Center, Chongqing 400016, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The blood-brain barrier (BBB) is the major obstacle limiting the reach of therapeutic drugs into the brain. Herein, an aspirin-based anti-inflammatory replenisher (aspCD) was fabricated by carbonizing aspirin to deliver drugs into the brain visually. The as-prepared aspCD combined the BBB-penetrating and anti-inflammatory effects of aspirin with the fluorescent and drug-loading properties of carbon dots (CDs), thereby delivering therapeutic drugs into the brain and acting as imaging agent as well as anti-inflammatory replenisher. In vivo experiments of mice and zebrafish revealed that fluorescence aspCD could effectively penetrate BBB. In vitro and in vivo inflammatory models demonstrated that aspCD could be regarded as an excellent anti-inflammatory replenisher. In addition, as a functional carrier, aspCD was proved to be capable of loading drugs with different polarity. In summary, carbonization of active precursors (therapeutic drugs) into CDs could be a promising strategy to achieve the loading and visualization of drugs as well as the retainment of their biological activities.

We developed aspirin-based fluorescence carbon dots (aspCD) to perform different functions. With the BBB-penetrating and anti-inflammatory effect of aspirin, aspCD could acted as an anti-inflammatory replenisher to alleviate inflammation in the brain; with the fluorescent and drug-loading properties of carbon dots, aspCD could deliver and visualize the therapeutics into the brain.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Neurological disorders including cerebral ischemia injury, Alzheimer, Parkinson, and brain tumors are major causes affecting human health, especially the elderly, because of their refractory [1,2]. One main reason they are so difficult to cure is that there exists a tight barrier in the central nervous system (CNS), that is blood-brain barrier (BBB). BBB separates the blood in the capillary and the interstitial fluid in the brain to maintain the optimal microenvironment and function of the CNS, such as brain homeostasis, central and peripheral signal transmission and monitoring of immune responses and pathogens [3–5]. But on the other hand, the tightly connected BBB would prevent the therapeutics reaching the brain, leading to poor therapeutic effect on nervous system diseases [6,7]. Therefore, it is urgent to explore suitable strategies for facilitating the therapeutics to penetrate BBB and enter the brain parenchyma.

To achieve this goal, researchers have made a lot of efforts. The most common approach is changing the way drugs are administrated to bypass the BBB, including intracerebroventricular administration [8,9], convection-enhanced delivery [10] and intrathecal administration [8,11]. Whereas, most of these methods are invasive so that some other potential risks must be taken into consideration such as the damage of normal tissues and infections [12]. Another relatively more mitigatory and straightforward strategy is to cross BBB directly by drug molecule modification or nanoparticles-mediated drug delivery, among which nanoparticles play a very important role. Nanoparticles have the advantages of easy surface functionalization and superior drug delivery capability [13,14]. The former makes it possible to be modified with BBB-targeted ligand, while the latter enables it to connect with drugs by absorption, dissolution, encapsulation, and covalent connection, and both of these jointly promote drug delivery to the CNS. Applying nanoparticles to achieve the brain targeted delivery of drugs has become an effective strategy [15–17]. In addition, nanoparticles tend to have certain biological effects on their own, such as ROS scavenging and anti-inflammation, which enable them to be applied for supplementary treatment of specific diseases. However, since most of the nanoparticles currently in use have difficulty in monitoring due to the lack of detectable signals, it is arduous to determine whether loaded drugs have reached the CNS before they work. Therefore, developing functional nanoparticles with detectable signals and specific activity to deliver therapeutic drugs into the brain visually and play auxiliary therapeutic functions is of great significance.

As a kind of novel carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have the advantages of good solubility [18], outstanding biocompatibility [19] and easy functionalization [20,21]. Moreover, CDs have great drug-loading potential to deliver therapeutic drugs due to their large specific surface area and rich surface functional groups [22,23]. CDs are also suitable for visualizing the drug distribution due to their controllable photoluminescence (PL) [24], high PL quantum yield [25], and excellent resistance to photobleaching [26]. In terms of BBB-penetrating, the size of CDs is <10 nm, which makes them possible to cross the 4–6 nm gaps between the endothelial cells of BBB, especially for CDs <4 nm [27]. More importantly, CDs could retain even strengthen the biological activity of their precursors [28]. For example, riboflavin-based carbon dots have much stronger singlet oxygen generation ability than that of riboflavin per se [29]. Our previous work also confirmed that arginine-based CDs remained its ability to produce nitric oxide [30,31]. In addition, researchers have showed that CDs based on glucose or tryptophan could effectively cross BBB because of the residual groups present on their surface [32,33]. Collectively, choosing precursor that can penetrate BBB to synthesize CDs with small size could be a promising strategy to achieve BBB penetration, and more likely to achieving the drug delivery and the further visualization of drug distribution in one structure. In addition, CDs based on therapeutic drugs may have similar therapeutic effects, Qu et al. proved that ibuprofen-based CDs had decent anti-inflammatory ability [28], some other groups confirmed antibiotic-based CDs could retain their antibacterial properties [34,35]. Therefore, drug-based CDs can be regarded as therapeutic replenishers in addition to drug carriers and bioimaging agents.

Herein, using aspirin as the precursor, our group fabricated aspirin-based anti-inflammatory replenisher (aspCD) which combined the BBB-penetrating and anti-inflammatory effects of aspirin with the fluorescent and drug-loading properties of CDs. Aspirin was selected as the precursor due to its superior anti-inflammatory effects and the ability to penetrate BBB [36–40]. The classical hydrothermal method was used by condensing aspirin under alkaline conditions to synthesize aspCD. On the one hand, benefiting from the superb fluorescence property of CDs, the as-prepared aspCD had the potential to be used for bioimaging. On the other hand, aspCD maintained the anti-inflammatory and BBB-penetrating biological activities of aspirin, enabling it a promising anti-inflammatory replenisher to alleviate inflammatory diseases in the brain. In addition, nanoscale aspCD could be considered as potential drug carrier to loaded various therapeutics. Altogether, a simple functional carrier and nanoreplenisher based on aspirin was successfully designed and fabricated to penetrate BBB for visualized drug delivery to the brain and bioimaging, as well as to cure the inflammatory diseases in the brain (Scheme 1 ).

Aspirin, sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and carrageenan were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) was obtained from Sango Biotech (Shanghai, China). Methanol and ethanol were purchased from ChengDu Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China). 4 % Paraformaldehyde Fix Solution were obtained from BBI Life Sciences (Shanghai, China). Escherichia coli LPS (serotype 055:B5) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium Nutriont Mixture F-12 (Ham) (DMEM/F-12) and RPMI 1640 were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, USA). Penicillin-streptomycin, SDS-PAGE gel preparing kit, PVDF membrane, non-fat powered milk, TBSTw (10×), SDS-PAGE electrophoresis buffer, western transfer buffer, TNF-α rabbit polyclonal antibody, IL 1β rabbit polyclonal antibody, β-actin rabbit monoclonal antibody and HRP-labeled Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG (H + L) were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Beijing, China). Certified Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) were obtained from Shanghai XP Biomed Ltd. (Viva Cell, Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The water used in this work was produced by an ultra-pure-water purification system of Research Water Purification Technology Co., Ltd. (Xiamen, China).

Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) and mouse hippocampal neuronal cell line (HT22) were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA). Murine macrophage cell line (RAW264.7), murine breast cancer 4 T1 cell line (4 T1) and human glioblastoma cell line (U87 MG) were obtained from the Cell Resource Center, Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. HT22 cells, U87 MG cells and 4 T1 cells were cultured in DMEM/F12 medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and streptomycin/penicillin and incubated at 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. HUVEC cells and RAW264.7 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and streptomycin/penicillin and incubated at 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

Healthy female BALB/c mice (15–20 g) were purchased from Laboratory Animal Center, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, China. The nude mice and C57BL/6j mice were purchased from Hunan SJA Laboratory Animal Co., Ltd. All animal experiments were performed according to guidelines evaluated and approved by the Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (SCXK2018–0003).

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra was collected on Model Ultra-6600A (Rigol, China). The PL spectra were recorded by a RF-5301 Spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was measured by the Nicolet iS5 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The basic morphology features of the as-prepared nanomaterials were characterized by JEM-1200EX TEM (JEOL, Japan) operating at an acceleration voltage of 120 kV (carbon-coated copper grid was dipped in the sample solution to deposit it on the film). Zeta potential was analyzed by Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, U.K.), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). MTT assay results were analyzed using Varioskan LUX microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). In vivo distribution of aspCD was studied using VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager (Vieworks, Korea). The expression of target proteins was photographed by an automatic chemiluminescence analysis system named Tanon-5200 (Tanon Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China). Intracellular fluorescence intensity was obtained on confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Leica, Germany). The tissue sections, H&E staining, and immunohistochemical staining were obtained from Wuhan Servicebio Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China).

The aspCD was prepared by a simple and universal hydrothermal method. Briefly, aspirin (2 g) was dissolved in basic solution and heated to 110 °C for 6 h with vigorous stirring in a round-bottomed flask. Then the solution was cooled to room temperature and purified using dialysis bag (MWCO 500 D) against water for 48 h, followed by lyophilizing to obtain the powder. The freeze-dried powder was collected for further characterization and application. The morphology and particle size of aspCD were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). XPS was used to analyze the element composition of aspCD. UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and FTIR spectroscopy were used to characterize the optical properties of aspCD.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of aspCD was calculated by comparative method according to revenant literature. Rhodamine B in ethanol solution was used as the standard, whose QY was reported to be 69 %. The QY value was calculated according to the following equation:
QY aspCD
=
QY RhB
∗
Grad aspCD
Grad RhB
∗
η aspCD 2
η RhB 2
where RhB represents rhodamine B, Grad is the gradient from the plot of fluorescence intensity vs absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent (rhodamine B was in ethanol solution and aspCD was in water). The absorbance of aspCD and rhodamine B in 10 mm quarts cuvette is not >0.1, so as to avoid the re-absorption effect.

Firstly, the fluorescence stability of aspCD in different medium was assessed. The powder was dissolved in PBS, RPMI 1640 cell culture medium and mice serum, respectively. Then the fluorescence spectra were measured and compared at 0 h and 24 h. After that, the fluorescence stability of aspCD under different conditions. To assess the influence of pH values on fluorescence stability, the aspCD solution was adjusted to pH 4–9, and then the fluorescence spectra were measured and the fluorescence intensity was compared. To assess the influence of ion strength values on fluorescence stability, the aspCD was dissolved in solution with the concentration of KCl ranging from 0 to 4 M, then the fluorescence spectra were measured and the fluorescence intensity was compared. To assess the photobleaching resistance of aspCD, the solution was exposed under a UV lamp for 0 to 5 h, respectively, then the fluorescence spectra were measured and the fluorescence intensity was compared.

HUVEC cells, HT22 cells, RAW264.7 cells, 4 T1 cells and U87 MG cells were used to assess the in vitro cytotoxicity of aspCD by MTT assay according to previous reports. The cells were seeded into a 96-well plate (5 × 103 per well) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, fresh culture medium (100 μL) containing different concentrations of aspirin and aspCD ranging from 0 to 1000 μg/mL was added after removing the culture medium and the cells were incubated for another 24 h. Afterward, in the dark, fresh culture medium (100 μL) containing MTT solution (10 μL, 5 mg/mL in PBS) was added and incubated for 4 h. Then, the medium was carefully removed and DMSO (150 μL) was added to lyse the cells. At 37 °C, the 96-well plate was gently shaken for 10 min in the dark. A microplate reader (Thermo) was employed to gauge the absorbance at 490 nm.

To evaluate the cell internalization and imaging ability of aspCD, HUVEC cells, HT22 cells, RAW264.7 cells, 4 T1 cells and U87 MG cells were seeded into a 24-well plate (5 × 104 per well) lined with a cover glass and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, the culture medium was replaced by fresh culture medium (500 μL) containing aspCD (200 μg/mL) and the cells were incubated for another 12 h. The blank culture medium (500 μL) was added as control. Afterward, the cells were washed with cold PBS three times and fixed with 4 % (w/v) formaldehyde for 20 min at room temperature and then washed with cold PBS twice. The cover glasses were then transferred to the microscope slides and observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) using a 405 nm excitation filter to obtain images.

All animal experiments were performed according to guidelines evaluated and approved by the Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (SCXK2018–0003). Six-week-old nude mice were used for in vivo fluorescence imaging. 100 μL of aspCD at 25 mg/kg and 100 μL of PBS were subcutaneously injected on the middle of back, then the mice were anesthetized and the fluorescence images were obtained by the VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager (Vieworks, Korea) under GFP channel (Ex: 390–490 nm; Em: 500–550 nm) at different time intervals.

To assess the in vivo distribution of aspCD, 100 μL of aspCD at 25 mg/kg and were intravenously injected. At different time intervals, the mice were sacrificed and brains and main organs were collected for ex vivo fluorescence imaging. To compared the distribution of aspCD in different organs more clearly, the organs at 24 h were chose for ex vivo imaging by the VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager. To further compare the ratio between the amount of aspCD injection and the amount of aspCD distribution in the organs, the fluorescence-concentration standard curve of aspCD was firstly established. Then, after intravenously injecting 100 μL aspCD at 10 mg/mL for 24 h, the main organs and brains were collected and homogenized in 2 mL PBS (Liver in 6 mL PBS). After that, the homogenate was centrifuged for 10 min at 12000 rpm, and the supernatant was taken to measure the fluorescence intensity to determine the concentration of aspCD.

Similarly, the zebrafish was also experimented to evaluate the distribution and the ability to cross the BBB of aspCD. The experiments on zebrafish were performed by Hangzhou Hunter Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Briefly, translucent Albino zebrafish (6 dpf) with melanin allele mutation were randomly selected and place in 6-well plates containing 20 zebrafish per well. AspCD at the dosage of 200 ng was administrated by microinjection, and PBS was also administrated as control group. After 6 h at 28 °C, 10 zebrafish in each group were randomly selected and observed under fluorescence microscope and the distribution of aspCD in the zebrafish was recorded and evaluated.

The anti-inflammatory effect of aspCD was compared with aspirin by examining the expression of TNF-α and IL-1β using western blot assay. Fresh 1640 culture medium (2 mL) containing 2 × 105 RAW264.7 cells per well were seeded into a 6-well plate and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, fresh culture medium (2 mL) containing aspCD and aspirin at the concentration of 0, 50 and 100 μg/mL were added into the well after removing the waste culture medium and incubated for 1 h, then lipopolysaccharides (LPS) at the final concentration of 500 ng/mL was added into each well. After 24 h, the cells were collected and lysed, and the proteins were extracted. Then the protein samples were denatured at 90 °C for 10 min. Next, the proteins of the samples were isolated by sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and transferred onto polyvinylidene fluoride microporous membranes (PVDF). Skim milk (5 %) was used to seal the proteins on the membranes, then the membranes were incubated with primary antibodies (1:1000 with TBST) overnight at 4 °C and incubated with secondary antibodies for 1 h. Finally, extreme hypersensitivity ECL chemiluminescence kit was used to develop the proteins and an automatic chemiluminescence analysis system named Tanon-5200 (Tanon Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China) was used to detect, photograph, and analyze the expression of target proteins.

The BALB/c mice were randomly divided into three groups, and 100 μL PBS, aspirin and aspCD were intravenously injected, respectively. After half an hour, 1 % carrageenan was subcutaneously injected on the back of the mice. After 24 h, the mice were sacrificed and the inflamed tissues and main organs were collected. Then the inflamed tissues were sectioned and stained with H&E to assess the anti-inflammatory effects of aspirin and aspCD. The main organs were also sectioned and stained with H&E to assess the biocompatibility of aspCD.

To make a more convincing case that aspCD could treat inflammatory diseases of the brain, we further established a brain inflammatory model according to previous reports [41,42]. The C57BL/6j mice were injected intraperitoneally with bacterial LPS (3 mg/kg) to establish brain inflammatory model. Immediately after the LPS injection, mice were administered with PBS, aspirin or aspCD (100 μL) at the dosage of 25 mg/kg. After 24 h, the mice were sacrificed and the brains were collected for immunohistochemistry assay.

The BALB/c mice were intravenously injected with 100 μL PBS and aspCD at the concentration of 25 mg/kg, respectively. After 24 h, the mice were sacrificed and the serum was collected to measure the level of alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), creatinine (CREA), urea nitrogen (BUN) and uric acid (UA).

To verify that aspCD could be utilized as excellent drug carriers, water-soluble rhodamine B (RhB), sightly water-soluble doxorubicin (DOX) and poorly water-solution rhein were used as model drugs. Firstly, aspCD, DOX, RhB and rhein were suspended in deionized water at the concentration of 10 mg/mL respectively, then 500 μL of DOX, RhB and rhein suspension were poured into 2 mL of aspCD solution respectively. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h, and then dialyzed with dialysis bags (MWCO 500 D) against water for 72 h to remove free drugs. At last, UV–vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer were utilized to determine whether the drugs were uploaded into aspCD.

Before measuring the release performance of aspCD, the fluorescence curves of RhB, DOX and rhein were established. Firstly, the drugs were formulated into solutions with different concentrations and their fluorescence was measured. Then, the fluorescence standard curves between the intensity and concentration of drugs were calculated. Subsequently, the dialysis method utilizing PBS as the medium was applied to estimate the release performance of aspCD at different pH values. Briefly, drug-loaded aspCD (aspCD@RhB, aspCD@DOX or aspCD@rhein, 1.0 mL) was transferred to a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000) and shaken in PBS (10 mL) at pH values of 5.5 and 7.4 at 37 °C and 300 rpm. Then, the release medium (1.0 mL) was collected from the bottle and PBS buffer (1.0 mL) was added to the corresponding bottle at predetermined intervals. Then, fluorescence spectroscopy was used to quantitatively measure the concentration of drugs in the samples.

In a typical synthesis, aspCD was prepared by hydrothermal method with slight modification in alkaline environment (NaOH), which is conductive to the dissolution and dispersion of aspirin due to ionization of -COOH to -COO−. The obtained aspCD solution was light-brown and the freeze-dried powder was white (Fig. 1a). The ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) spectra in Fig. S1 showed that aspirin in alkaline solution had two peaks, π-π peak at 230 nm and n-π peak at 295 nm, the absorbance of aspCD was similar with that of pure aspirin, indicating that the carbonization process didn't affect the conjugated structure of aspirin, for which reason aspCD could retain most of the properties of aspirin such as the potential to pass BBB and anti-inflammatory effect. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 1b showed aspCD exhibited a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation, and the particle size analysis (Fig. 1c) from TEM image showed the size of aspCD was ~1.90 nm, which was consistent with conventional CDs (<10 nm) [43]. The ultrasmall size is suitable to cross the gaps (4–6 nm) between endothelial cells of BBB [27]. Then, the results in Fig. 1d showed that zeta potential dropped from 2.3 ± 0.2 mV to −19.5 ± 0.2 mV after carbonizing aspirin into aspCD, which was the result of carbonizing the carbon-based material into carboxyl groups. While dissolving aspCD in PBS solution, its zeta potential rose slightly to −10.6 ± 1.2 mV, which was the results of partial neutralization of carboxyl groups in aspCD by PBS buffer. The negative charge of aspCD is conductive to its stable presence in circulation since the surface of cell membrane in the blood is negatively charged and homogeneous charges repel each other.

Afterwards, the structure analysis of aspCD was carried out by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FTIR spectra was shown in Fig. 1e, aspirin exhibited CO stretching vibration of acetyl and carboxyl at 1755 cm−1 and 1690 cm−1, after forming aspCD, the CO stretching vibration of acetyl and carboxyl was partially retained and shifted a little to 1626 cm−1 and 1591 cm−1. The anti-inflammatory effect of aspirin depends mainly on the acetylation of the serine residue of COX-1 or COX-2 by its acetyl group, thereby blocking the access of fatty acid substrate to oxidate the active site. Therefore, the acetyl groups on aspCD made it possible to retain the anti-inflammatory activity of aspirin. The XPS results in Fig. S2a displayed the C peak at 284 eV and the O peak at 530 eV, indicating that C and O were the main elements of aspCD, which was consistent with aspirin. Fig. S2b and S2c were the C1s and O1s spectra of aspCD, respectively. The C1s spectrum was consist with three main peaks: CC peak at 284.1 eV, CO peak at 285.5 eV, CO peak at 287.9 eV. The O1s spectrum could be divided into three peaks at 530.7 eV, 532.2 eV and 535.4 eV, which were contributed to HO-C=O, H2O, and -C=O-O−, respectively. The presence of these bonds confirmed the existence of carboxyl and acetyl groups, which were consistent with the result of FTIR, proving that the functional group acetyl, which is the origin of the anti-inflammatory effects, was retained.

The fluorescence property of aspCD was measured since it determines the application in biological imaging. The emission spectra of aspCD at different excitation wavelengths in Fig. 1f displayed the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, which was the typical characteristic of fluorescence carbon materials. Since 365 nm was the most commonly used wavelength for UV lamp and the inset photograph was taken under a 365 nm UV lamp, we chose 365 nm as the excitation wavelength to measure the emission spectrum of aspCD and found it focused on 410 nm. Then emitted at 400 nm, we found that the excitation wavelength of aspCD was located at 340 nm. Then we used RhB ethanol solution as the reference to determine the fluorescence quantum yield of aspCD. According to the PL vs Abs. curves of aspCD aqueous solution and RhB ethanol solution in Fig. S3, the fluorescence quantum yield of aspCD was calculated to be 21 % (Table 1 ). Then, the time-correlated single-photon counting assay was tested at the excitation of 365 nm to further analyze the origin of the fluorescence of aspCD. Fig. 1h showed there were two different lifetime components of aspCD, 2.34 ns of 59.14 % and 4.43 ns of 40.86 %, and the average lifetime was 3.19 ns, which implied that aspCD had two fluorescence centers. The fluorescence center with 2.34 ns of lifetime was a surface state, which led to the excitation-dependent fluorescence property, while the second fluorescence center with 4.43 ns of lifetime was a carbon core center. These results demonstrated that fluorescence CDs based on aspirin entitled aspCD was successfully fabricated.

To ensure that aspCD can be utilized for stable imaging, its fluorescence stability was investigated. Firstly, the fluorescence of aspCD in different mediums was evaluated, Fig. 2a-c showed that PBS, RPMI 1640 cell culture medium and serum had little fluorescence themselves (excited at 365 nm), after co-incubating aspCD with PBS, RPMI 1640 cell culture medium or serum for 24 h, the fluorescence properties of aspCD were well preserved in view of the unchanged fluorescence peak positions and intensity. Then, the fluorescence intensity of aspCD at different pH values, ion strengths, and irradiation times were measured. The results showed that the fluorescence peak and intensity of aspCD remained nearly unchanged at different pH values (pH 4–9) (Fig. 2d and g), ion strengths (the concentration of KCl from 0 to 2.0 M) (Fig. 2e and h) and irradiation times (0–5 h) (Fig. 2f and i). Together, all these results displayed that aspCD have superb fluorescence and fluorescence stability, which is the foundation of biological imaging.

The MTT assay was performed to evaluate the toxicity of aspCD. The inflammatory mouse leukemic monocyte macrophage (RAW264.7), normal human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC), murine mammary cancer cells (4 T1), hippocampal neuronal cell (HT22) and human glioblastoma cells (U87 MG) were used to assess the cytotoxicity and bioimaging capabilities of aspCD. As shown in Fig. S4, even at the high concentration of 1000 μg/mL, after incubating aspCD with cells for 24 h, the cell viabilities of HUVEC, HT22, U87 MG, RAW264.7 and 4 T1 cells remained above 80 %, 80 %, 70 %, 60 % and 50 %, respectively. These results indicated that aspCD had relatively good biocompatibility for a variety of types of cells. It's worth noting that aspCD and aspirin displayed slightly stronger killing effect on tumor cells (4 T1 and U87 MG cells) and inflammatory cells (RAW264.7 cells) than normal cells (HUVEC and HT22 cells), which were consistent with previous reports that aspirin had mighty anti-inflammatory effect and efficient antitumor effect [44–47].

The cell imaging capability of fluorescent aspCD in different cells was assessed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 3a and Fig. S5, aspCD could be effectively internalized by inflammatory cells (RAW264.7), normal endothelial cells (HUVEC), peripheric tumor cells (4 T1), nerve cells (HT22) and central tumor cells (U87 MG) to image them clearly after incubating for 24 h at the concentration of 200 μg/mL. Moreover, the fluorescence of aspCD could be found in both cytoplasm and nucleus, and there showed no difference in distribution among different cell lines, indicating that the distribution of aspCD had no suborganelle specificity or cell specificity, which may be the result of the ultrasmall particle size of aspCD. Altogether, the as-prepared aspCD had outstanding biocompatibility and possessed the potential as imaging agent for various cells.

After that, whether aspCD could be utilized for in vivo imaging was evaluated by a VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager (Vieworks, Korea). Fig. S6 showed aspCD solution exhibited strong fluorescence under GFP channel (Ex: 390–490 nm; Em: 500–550 nm), which underlies the application of aspCD for in vivo imaging. Then the nude mice were subcutaneously injected with aspCD and the fluorescence signals were detected at different time intervals. The image before injection was regarded as 0 min as control. Fig. 3b showed the fluorescence at injection site increased gradually and reached the maximum at 40 min after injection, then reduced rapidly and disappeared in 2 h. The disappearance of fluorescence could be attributed to either elimination by the body or infiltration into deep tissues that cannot be monitored. These results demonstrated that aspCD could be suitable for in vivo imaging.

The distribution of aspCD in brain and main organs was monitored by a VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager (Vieworks, Korea). The results in Fig. S7 showed aspCD mainly distributed to liver within 1 h and to brain within 2 h, and lasted for >24 h. In order to compare the distribution of aspCD in different organs more clearly, mice were sacrificed and main organs were collected for ex vivo imaging after intravenously injecting PBS or aspCD at the concentration of 25 mg/kg for 24 h. The results were shown in Fig. 4b, compared to control group (PBS), the liver of aspCD group had much brighter fluorescence, which can be explained as the nonspecific liver accumulation of nanoparticles. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of brain in aspCD group was much stronger than that in control group (PBS), substantiating that aspCD preserved the BBB-penetrating ability of aspirin. Its quantitative analysis result was shown as Fig. 4d. To further determine the absolute amount of aspCD distributed in brain and main organs and its ratio in injection dosage, the standard fluorescence curve of aspCD was firstly measured, the results in Fig. S8 displayed that the fluorescence intensity and concentration of aspCD showed a good linear relationship with R2 at 0.99879. Then the fluorescence intensity of supernatant homogenized from brain and organs was determined. The results in Fig. 4e showed that the amount of aspCD in brain was about 28.13 μg and its ratio was about 2.81 % of the injection dosage, which is much higher than most drug [6,48].

It's known that aspirin had the ability to pass through the BBB [37–40], therefore, to confirm that aspCD retained the BBB penetrability, the fluorescence intensity in the brains was compared separately. The temporal distribution of aspCD in the brain was monitored in Fig. 4a and Fig. S9. The results showed that aspCD distributed in brain in 2 h and its distribution reached maximum at 12 h and lasted for >24 h. Fig. 4c showed the strong fluorescence could be observed throughout the whole brain, indicating the nonspecific distribution of aspCD in the brain. Then the brains were sectioned and the fluorescence was observed with CLSM. The results also showed a nonspecific whole-brain distribution of aspCD. To further verify the BBB penetrability of aspCD more intuitively in vivo, the zebrafish model was used to examine the fluorescence distribution of aspCD. Fig. 4f showed the zebrafish in aspCD group exhibited much stronger fluorescence than that in control group (PBS), and the fluorescence was mainly located in the liver due to the liver enrichment effect of nanoparticles. It's worth noting that there was also quite a bit of fluorescence in the brain, revealing that a significant amount of aspCD traveled through the BBB to the brain parenchyma, which was consistent with the results in mice model. Altogether, these results suggested that aspCD retained the BBB-penetrating ability of aspirin, which could be the foundation to cure the neurological disorders. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of aspCD could be used to visualize its distribution in the brain.

As a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug, aspirin possesses mighty anti-inflammatory effect. Therefore, aspCD may retained this effect so that it can be used as therapeutic replenisher. To verify this, the expression of TNF-α and IL-1β, two main inflammatory factors, was measured by western blot (WB) assay after lipopolysaccharides (LPS) challenge. LPS is an endotoxin produced by Gram-negative bacteria and can induce strong inflammatory response in RAW264.7 cells [49]. TNF-α and IL-1β are secreted by activated macrophages during the acute phase of inflammatory response, therefore, the expression level of TNF-α and IL-1β can reflect the degree of inflammatory response to a certain extent [50]. The result in Fig. 5a showed the expression of TNF-α and IL-1β in both aspCD and aspirin groups was much lower than that in control group (PBS), and the anti-inflammatory activity of aspCD could reach the same level as that of aspirin. Afterwards, the in vivo anti-inflammatory effect of aspCD was revealed using a carrageenan-induced acute inflammatory model (Fig. 5b). As shown in Fig. 5c, there were plenty of neutrophil infiltrates in subcutaneous tissues of the control group (PBS), indicating that carrageenan could effectively cause tissue inflammation. However, the neutrophil infiltrates were much less in aspirin and aspCD groups. Surprisingly, aspCD showed much stronger anti-inflammatory effect, which might be the result of the increasing solubility of aspCD than aspirin. Altogether, aspCD acted as a nanoreplenisher with superior anti-inflammation activity.

To make a more convincing case that aspCD could treat inflammatory diseases of the brain, the brain inflammatory model was established. After 24 h treatment with PBS, aspirin or aspCD (Fig. 6a), the results in Fig. 6b showed that the expression of IL-1β and TNF-α was decreased in both aspirin and aspCD-treated groups. The quantitative results in Fig. 6c showed the expression of IL-1β in aspirin group and aspCD group was 0.16 and 0.15 fold of that in PBS group, respectively. Similarly, the expression of TNF-α in aspirin group and aspCD group was 0.35 and 0.30 fold of that in PBS group. Those results demonstrated that aspCD had strong anti-inflammatory activity similar to aspirin against inflammatory diseases of the brain.

As a kind of novel nanomaterials, CDs have attracted great attention in drug delivery recently. To verify that aspCD could be utilized as excellent drug carrier, water-soluble RhB, sightly water-soluble doxorubicin (DOX) and poorly water-solution rhein were used as model drugs. Fig. 7a showed the process to load model drugs onto aspCD. Fig. 7b showed that the characteristic peaks of aspCD around 230 nm and 295 nm and the absorbance peak of RhB located at ~554 nm could be detected after loading RhB into aspCD. The fluorescence spectra in Fig. 7c showed aspCD@RhB possessed both the fluorescence peaks of aspCD around 410 nm (excited at 340 nm) and RhB around 574 nm (excited at 507 nm). Similarly, aspCD@DOX had both the characteristic UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence peaks of aspCD and DOX (Fig. 7d and e), aspCD@rhein had both the characteristic UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence peaks of aspCD and rhein (Fig. 7f and g). These results confirmed that aspCD could be utilized as an excellent carrier to load agents with different water-solubility.

To evaluate the release of loaded drugs (RhB, DOX and rhein) from aspCD at different pH values, we established the standard fluorescence curve of free drugs, and then measured the fluorescence of drugs released from aspCD at different times. As shown in Fig. S10 the fluorescence intensity and concentration of free drugs showed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9901 for RhB, R2 = 0.99225 for DOX, R2 = 0.99904 for rhein). Fig. 7h-j showed the release kinetics of different drugs at different pH values. Fig. 7h showed that the release of RhB is independent of pH, the drug release curves of RhB at pH 7.4 and pH 5.5 were almost the same. Fig. 7i demonstrated that DOX could be released more in acidic environment (pH 5.5). Fig. 7j showed that rhein could hardly be released, while it can be rapidly released under weakly alkaline conditions (pH 7.4), in which condition rhein can dissociate to increase its solubility. Obviously, although aspCD could load drugs with different polarity, its drug release property was not the same. DOX can be released slowly in weakly acidic conditions (pH 5.5) in the body without being released in the blood (pH 7.4) so that aspCD can deliver DOX to treat diseases with acidic microenvironment, such as tumor. However, RhB and rhein can be rapidly released in blood (pH 7.4) so that aspCD is not suitable for delivering drugs like RhB and rhein. Therefore, the drug release properties of aspCD mainly depended on the loaded drugs, it's important to choose suitable drugs.

As last, we assessed the histocompatibility of aspCD, the H&E analysis results in Fig. S11 indicated that there was no obvious toxicity of aspCD to heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney after intravenous injection. In addition, the acute toxicity of aspCD in mice was assessed by measuring the liver and kidney functions. As shown in Fig. S12, there were no obvious difference of ALT, AST, ALP, CREA, BUN, and UA in PBS group and aspCD group, implying the distinguished in vivo safety to liver and kidney. These results demonstrated that aspCD had good biocompatibility, which is extremely beneficial for its application in vivo.

In summary, we successfully synthesized a fluorescence carbon dot replenisher based on aspirin (aspCD) by directly heating aspirin in alkaline solution. The basic characterization revealed the morphology, surface, composition, and optical properties of aspCD. The admirable and stable fluorescence of aspCD is extremely conductive for monitoring its distribution and for biological imaging. Meanwhile, in vitro and in vivo experiments proved that aspCD had excellent biosafety, while remained the strong anti-inflammatory effect and BBB penetrability of aspirin. In addition, the as-prepared aspCD could act as a functional carrier, which can be loaded with drugs of different polarity. Altogether, the strategy of carbonizing active precursors in this work provides a reference to construct nanoreplenisher for loading therapeutic drugs as well as performing certain biological functions.
Xianming Zhang: Conceptualization; Methodology; Formal analysis; Investigation; Writing - Original Draft; Visualization.
Qinghua Yu: Methodology; Visualization; Formal analysis.
Ping Zhou: Investigation.
Shiyu Yang: Methodology.
Jiashan Xia: Resources.
Tao Deng: Conceptualization; Formal analysis; Resources; Writing - Review & Editing; Supervision; Funding acquisition.
Chao Yu: Conceptualization; Formal analysis; Resources; Writing - Review & Editing; Supervision; Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
